storm.summary_layperson: Thunderstorms are expected this evening — none on radar yet, but NWS is forecasting severe weather with a 90% chance of rain, and the Sound water is too warm today to provide the usual coastal buffer.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Sound water (71°F) is nearly as warm as the air (+2.7°F delta), so there is no marine stable layer to weaken incoming storms — regional forecasts apply at face value.
storm.detail_html:
OKX forecasters are calling for scattered severe thunderstorms and flash flooding this afternoon and evening. Radar as of 4:20 PM shows no cells — convective initiation has not fired yet — but the environment is primed. The NWS forecast carries 90% precipitation probability and 1–2 inches of potential rainfall; the primary threat per the OKX Area Forecast Discussion is damaging wind gusts. A second round of storms is also possible late Tuesday into Wednesday.

Instability is building through the window. HRRR shows surface CAPE of 240 J/kg at 4 PM rising to 660 J/kg by 8 PM and 1,180 J/kg by 9 PM. OKX forecasters cite a minimum of 1,000–1,500 J/kg of MUCAPE expected, with ensemble maxima reaching 2,000–3,000 J/kg possible in the metro area. Bulk wind shear is 41 kt from the west — well above the 35 kt level where organized severe convection maintains intensity. One key uncertainty: smoke from an ongoing Air Quality Alert may limit surface heating and delay destabilization; OKX notes the exact hazard locations may not become clear until a few hours before onset.

The cove has no local protective buffer today. The Long Island Sound water at 71°F is only 2.7°F cooler than inland air — far below the 10°F minimum needed to create a meaningful marine stable layer. The coastal stabilization mechanism is inactive. Storms arriving from the southwest will cross warm water with no attenuation, and regional NWS warnings should be taken at face value for Riverside rather than assumed to be diminished by the usual coastal effect.

If convection fires to the west (the NWS scenario), strong westerly shear will drive organized cells toward the coast. The risk escalates through the window: moderate now with no cells on radar, but building toward the upper end of moderate by 9–10 PM as CAPE peaks. What would raise the risk to high: cells appearing within 25 miles at 45+ dBZ tracking toward the cove, an active severe thunderstorm warning, or an upstream station spiking to 35+ kt as a gust-front preview. What keeps it at moderate: no cells yet, and smoke suppression may limit the worst-case destabilization scenario.

wind.regime_label: Moderate gradient wind — no sea breeze in play

hourly.shift_note: clocking back S near sunset, storm risk rising
rebuilding SW, thunderstorm risk at peak

wind.tactics_blurb: South gradient wind aligns cleanly with the cove's north-south opening — pressure is fairly uniform with a slight edge on the right (west shore) as the direction backs toward SSW/SW mid-window. Race early: the 17:00–18:00 slot has the best breeze at 12 kt S, and the storm clock runs hot after 21:00.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
This is a moderate gradient wind day — no sea breeze in play. The inland–Sound temperature difference (73°F inland vs 71°F water) is only 2.7°F, well below the 5°F threshold needed for thermal sea-breeze development. All stations from Block Island to Westchester show unified southerly gradient flow: KBOS at SSW 14 kt gusting 19, KBDR at S 9 kt gusting 15, KHVN at S 12 kt gusting 17. This is a synoptic southerly setup driven by the regional pressure pattern — not a thermally-driven sea breeze — and the sea-breeze direction-maturation correction does not apply.

HRRR forecasts 6–12 kt during the 17:00–22:00 window, peaking at 5 PM (12 kt S) and lightest at 7 PM (6 kt SW). Direction oscillates between S and SW (183–221°) through the window, consistent with unsettled pre-frontal flow rather than the clean periodic oscillations of a mature sea breeze. HRRR and the west-LIS station median diverge by only 2°, so directional confidence is high. Note that RYC is currently reading a 4-kt ten-minute average against HRRR's 8–9 kt at this grid point — the sheltered cove tends to run somewhat lighter than the open-water model forecast. Expect actual racing conditions closer to the 4–8 kt range for much of the window.

South wind is the cleanest direction for the cove, blowing straight up its north-south axis with uniform pressure across the course. When the direction backs to SSW around 6–7 PM, there will be slight channeling along the west shore, giving a marginal pressure advantage on the right side. Direction agreement across the ensemble is 0.79 — coherent but not perfectly tight, with a few inland stations showing scattered directions typical of light pre-storm boundary layer flow. Play the shifts rather than committing hard to either bank.

Because this is a gradient wind day, there is no sunset collapse — the gradient holds through and beyond 22:00 regardless of the sun going down around 8:20 PM. HRRR's extended outlook (10 PM onward) shows mean speeds of 11 kt, confirming the gradient persists. However, the thunderstorm clock competes directly: HRRR CAPE rises from near-zero at 5 PM to 1,180 J/kg by 9 PM. If a squall line fires upstream, pre-frontal flow could surge temporarily, followed by gusty westerly winds with line passage.

The tactical summary: race early. The 17:00–18:00 slot offers the best combination of reliable breeze (12 → 8 kt S/SSW) and lowest storm risk. The 19:00 hour is the lightest and most directionally variable. The 20:00+ period carries the highest convective exposure as CAPE peaks — if racing continues past 20:00, keep a sharp eye on the western horizon and have a plan to get off the water quickly.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 stale (last reading 75 min ago — obs from 3:15 PM EDT)
RAP CAPE unavailable; storm instability estimates from OKX AFD as fallback
Smoke from Air Quality Alert (through midnight) may suppress afternoon destabilization — storm onset timing uncertain per OKX AFD
